questions.q: If the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>x</mi><mi>tan</mi><mi>θ</mi></math> cuts the curve <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>3</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mi>x</mi><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> at the points <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>O</mi><mi>A</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>O</mi><mi>B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>O</mi><mi>C</mi></math> are in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>HP</mi></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>tan</mi><mi>θ</mi></math> is equal to
questions.solution:
Given,

line y=xtanθ cuts the curve x3+xy2+2x2+2y2+3x+1=0   …i at three points A,B and C.

Let us assume Ax1,x1tanθ, Bx2,x2tanθ and Cx3,x3tanθ

⇒OA=x12+x12 tan2θ

=x11+tan2θ=x1secθ

Similarly OB=x2secθ and OC=x3secθ

∵OA,OB,OC in H.P.

⇒OB=2·OAOCOA+OC

⇒x2·secθ=2x1·x3sec2θx1+x3secθ

⇒x1x2+x2x3=2x1x3

⇒x1x2+x2x3+x3x1=3x1x3   …ii.

Now, substitute y=xtanθ in equation i, we get

x3+x3tan2θ+2x2+2x2tan2θ+3x+1=0

⇒x3sec2θ+2x2sec2θ+3x+1=0   …iii

Now, the roots of the equation iii will be x1,x2,x3.

⇒x1·x2+x2·x3+x3·x1=3sec2θ   …iv

⇒x1·x2·x3=-1sec2θ   …v 

From equations ii and iv, 3x1x3=3sec2θ

⇒x1x3=1sec2θ   …vi

From equations v and vi, x2=-1

∵x2 is one of the roots of the equation iii, so it will satisfy the equation.

⇒-sec2θ+2sec2θ-3+1=0

⇒sec2θ=2

⇒secθ=±2

⇒tanθ=±1.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.q: The line <math><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>c</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac></math> divides the triangle with vertices <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>0,0</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mi></mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>1,1</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>9,1</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> into two regions. If the area of the two regions is the same, then <math><mi>c</mi></math> is equal to
questions.solution:
2⋅ 1 2 ⋅ 9 1 1 c 2 1 1 c 2 c 18 1 = 1 2 9 1 1 1 1 1 0 0 1 = 1 2 ⋅8=4
⇒c2=3 or c2=15 (not possible)

⇒c=6

questions.q: A line <math><mn>4</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> passes thought the point <math><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>7</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi></mrow></mfenced></math> and meets the line <math><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> at <math><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> whose equation is <math><mn>3</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>. If the equation of line <math><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> such that <math><mi></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mo>=</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is <math><mfrac><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi></mrow></mfrac><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math>, then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math> is equal to
questions.solution:
tan⁡θ=m1-m21+m1m2=-4-341-3=198
198=m-341+3m4
m=-4 or -5289
Equation AC is 52x+89y+519=0
⇒a=-51952

questions.q: The equations <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>±</mo><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> are the sides of
questions.solution:
Equation of OA is y=3x

⇒m=tanθ=3⇒θ=60° 

Equation of OB is y=-3x

So ∠XOB=120° and ∠BOA=60°

Equation of AB is y=1

Now points A13,1 & B-13,1

So OA=23=OB⇒∠ABO=∠BAO=60°

So, ΔOAB is an equilateral triangle.

questions.q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mfenced><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>B</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> be given three points. A line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>m</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>m</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn></math> , intersects lines <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>C</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi><mi>C</mi></math> at point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> respectively. Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>A</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>A</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> be the areas of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Δ</mi><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi><mi>C</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Δ</mi><mi>P</mi><mi>Q</mi><mi>C</mi></math> respectively, such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>A</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><msub><mi>A</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>, then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> is equal to :
questions.solution:
P≡x1,mx1

 Q≡x2,mx2

A1=12341201-111=132

A2=12x1mx11x2mx21201

A2=122mx1-mx2=mx1-x2

A1=3 A2⇒132=3 mx1-x2

⇒  x1-x2=166 m

AC: x+3 y=2

BC: y=4 x-8

P:x+3y=2 & y=mx⇒x1=21+3m

Q: y=4x-8 & y=mx⇒x2=84-m

x1-x2=21+3m-84-m

=-26 m1+3m4-m=26 m3m+1m-4

=26 m3m+14-m

x1-x2=136 m

26 m3m+14-m=136 m

⇒  12m2=-3m+1m-4

⇒  12m2=-3m2-11m-4

⇒  15m2-11m-4=0

⇒  15m2-15m+4m-4=0

⇒  15m+4m-1=0

⇒  m=1
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.q: The line 2x+3y=12 meets the coordinates axes at A and B respectively. The line through 5,5 perpendicular to AB meets the coordinate axes and the line AB at C, D and E respectively. If O is the origin, then the area (in sq. units) of the figure OCEB is equal to
questions.solution:
The equation of a line passing through P5,5 and perpendicular to 2x+3y=12 is
y-5=32x-5
or, 3x-2y-5=0
The coordinates of the point of intersection of 2x+3y-12=0 and 3x-2y-5=0 are E3,2.
The line 3x-2y-5=0 meets x-axis at C53,0.
Now,
Area of figure OCEB
=Area of ΔAOB-Area of ΔACE
=12OA×OB-12AC×EL
=12×6×4-12×133×2
=233 sq. units
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The orthocentre and centroid of triangle are $A(-3,5)$ and $B(3,3)$ respectively. If $C$ is the circumcenter of this triangle, then the radius of the circle having line segment $A C$ as a diameter, is units.
questions.solution:
Circumcentre divides orthocentre $A(-3,0)$ and centroid $B(3,3)$ externally in the ratio 3:1
Hence, $C \equiv\left(\frac{3 \times 3-1 \times(-3)}{3-1}, \frac{3 \times 3-1 \times 5}{3-1}\right) \equiv(6,2)$
Now required radius $=\frac{1}{2} A C=\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{(6+3)^2+(2-5)^2}=3 \sqrt{\frac{5}{2}}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
If $p_{1}$ and $p_{2}$ are the lengths of perendiculars from the origin to the lines
$x \sin \theta+y \cos \theta=5 \cos 2 \theta$ and $x \operatorname{cosec} \theta+y \sec \theta-5=0$ respectively, then
$p_{1}^{2}+4 p_{2}^{2}=$
questions.solution:
As per condition given, we write
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{p}_{1} &=\frac{|-5 \cos 2 \theta|}{\sqrt{\sin ^{2} \theta+\cos ^{2} \theta}} \text { and } p_{2}=\frac{|-5|}{\sqrt{\operatorname{cosec}^{2} \theta+\sec ^{2} \theta}} \\
\therefore \mathrm{p}_{1}^{2}=\frac{25 \cos ^{2} 2 \theta}{1} \text { and } 4 \mathrm{p}_{2}^{2} &=\frac{4(25)}{\operatorname{cosec}^{2} \theta+\sec ^{2} \theta} \\
\therefore \mathrm{p}_{1}^{2}+4 \mathrm{p}_{2}^{2} &=25\left(\cos ^{2} \theta-\sin ^{2} \theta\right)^{2}+\frac{100}{\left(\frac{1}{\sin ^{2} \theta}\right)+\frac{1}{\cos ^{2} \theta}} \\
&=25(\cos \theta-\sin \theta)^{2}(\cos \theta+\sin \theta)^{2}+100 \sin ^{2} \theta \cos ^{2} \theta \\
&=25(1-\sin 2 \theta)(1+\sin 2 \theta)+25\left(4 \sin ^{2} \theta \cos ^{2} \theta\right) \\
&=25\left(1-\sin 2 \theta+\sin 2 \theta-\sin ^{2} 2 \theta\right)+25(2 \sin \theta \cos \theta)^{2} \\
&=25\left(1-\sin ^{2} 2 \theta\right)+25(\sin 2 \theta)^{2} \\
&=25\left(\cos ^{2} 2 \theta\right)+25\left(\sin ^{2} 2 \theta\right)=25
\end{aligned}
$$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The maximum value of z = 6x +8y subject to <math><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>≥</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>≤</mo><mn>12</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>≥</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>≥</mo><mn>0</mn></math> is…..
questions.solution:
We have, z = 6x +8y
Subject to constraints x-y≥0,x+3y≤12,x≥0,y≥0 .
On taking given constraints as equations, we get the following graph

Intersecting point of the line x-y=0 and x+3y=12 is B (3, 3).
Here, OABO is the required feasible region
Whose corner points are O (0, 0), A (12, 0) and B (0, 4) Now,
Corner points Z=6x+8y
O (0, 0) 6×0+8×0=0
A (12, 0) 6×12+8×0=72
(maximum)
B (3, 3) 6×3+8×3=42
∴ Maximum value of Z is 72.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The number of integral values of $p$ in the domain $[-5,5]$, such that the equation $2 x^2+4 x y-\mathrm{p} y^2+4 x+\mathrm{q} y+1=0$ represents pair of lines, are
questions.solution:
Given equation of pair of lines is
$2 x^2+4 x y-p y^2+4 x+q y+1=0$
Comparing with $\mathrm{a} x^2+2 \mathrm{~h} x y+\mathrm{b} y^2+2 \mathrm{~g} x+2 \mathrm{f} y+\mathrm{c}=0$, we get $\mathrm{a}=2, \mathrm{~h}=2, \mathrm{~b}=-\mathrm{p}$
If the given equation represents a pair of straight lines, then
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{h}^2 \geq \mathrm{ab} \\
& \Rightarrow 4 \geq-2 \mathrm{p} \\
& \Rightarrow 2 \geq-\mathrm{p} \\
& \Rightarrow \mathrm{p} \geq-2
\end{aligned}$
$\therefore \quad$ Possible values of $\mathrm{p}$ from domain $[-5,5]$ are $-2,-1,0,1,2,3,4,5$.
$\therefore \quad$ Number of integral values of $p=8$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: If the axes are rotated through an angle $45^{\circ}$ in the positive direction without changing the origin, then the co-ordinates of the point $(\sqrt{2}, 4)$ in the old system are
questions.solution:
If $\theta$ is the angle of rotation, then the co-ordinates in the new system are $x^{\prime}=x \cos \theta+y \sin \theta$, $y^{\prime}=y \cos \theta-x \sin \theta$
Given that $x^{\prime}=\sqrt{2}, y^{\prime}=4$
Thus, $x \cos \theta+y \sin \theta=\sqrt{2}$
$$
y \cos \theta-x \sin \theta=4
$$
Also, $\theta=\frac{\pi}{4} \Rightarrow x \cos \frac{\pi}{4}+y \sin \frac{\pi}{4}=\sqrt{2}$
and $y \cos \frac{\pi}{4}-x \sin \frac{\pi}{4}=4$
$$
\begin{array}{ll}
\Rightarrow & x+y=2 \\
\text { and } & y-x=4 \sqrt{2}
\end{array}
$$
On adding Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get $2 y=2+4 \sqrt{2}$ $\Rightarrow \quad y=1+2 \sqrt{2}$
On subtracting (i) and (ii), we get
$$
\begin{array}{rlrl} 
& & 2 x & =2-4 \sqrt{2} \\
& x & =1-2 \sqrt{2}
\end{array}
$$
$$
\Rightarrow \quad x=1-2 \sqrt{2}
$$
Thus, the co-ordinates of $(\sqrt{2}, 4)$ in the old system $(1-2 \sqrt{2}, 1+2 \sqrt{2})$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: If a straight line perpendicular to $2 x-3 y+7=0$ forms a triangle with the co-ordinate axes whose area is 3 sq. units, then the equation of the straight line is :
questions.solution:
The given line is $2 x-3 y+7=0$
A perpendicular line to given line is $$ 3 x+2 y+k=0 $$ Put $y=0 \Rightarrow 3 x+k=0$ $$ x=-\frac{k}{3} $$ Put $x=0 \Rightarrow 0+2 y+k=0$ $$ y=-\frac{k}{2} $$ Area of the triangle $O A B=\frac{1}{2} \cdot O A \cdot O B$ $$ \begin{aligned} \Rightarrow & 3 & =\frac{1}{2} \cdot\left(-\frac{k}{3}\right)\left(-\frac{k}{2}\right) \\ \Rightarrow & k^2 & =36 \Rightarrow k= \pm 6 \end{aligned} $$ Putting in (i) $$ 3 x+2 y= \pm 6 $$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The transformed equation of $3 x^2+3 y^2+2 x y=2$, when the coordinate axes are rotated through an angle of $45^{\circ}$, is
questions.solution:
Since, the axes are rotated through an angle $45^{\circ}$, then we replace $(x, y)$ by $\left(x \cos 45^{\circ}-y \sin 45^{\circ}, x \sin 45^{\circ}+y \cos 45^{\circ}\right)$ ie, $\quad\left(\frac{x}{\sqrt{2}}-\frac{y}{\sqrt{2}}, \frac{x}{\sqrt{2}}+\frac{y}{\sqrt{2}}\right)$ in the given equation $3 x^2+3 y^2+2 x y=2$
$$
\begin{array}{rrr}
\therefore \quad 3\left(\frac{x}{\sqrt{2}}-\frac{y}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2+3\left(\frac{x+y}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2 \\
& +2\left(\frac{x-y}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\left(\frac{x+y}{\sqrt{2}}\right)=2 \\
\Rightarrow & \frac{3}{2}\left(x^2+y^2+2 x y\right)+\frac{3}{2}\left(x^2+y^2-2 x y\right) \\
& & +\frac{2}{2}\left(x^2-y^2\right)=2 \\
\Rightarrow & 4 x^2+2 y^2=2 \\
\Rightarrow & 2 x^2+y^2=1
\end{array}
$$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The line joining the points $A(2,0)$ and $B(3,1)$ is rotated through an angle of $45^{\circ}$, about $A$ in the anti-clockwise direction. The coordinates of $B$ in the new position
questions.solution:
Slope of $A B=\frac{1-0}{3-2}=1$ Therefore, $\angle B A X=45^{\circ}$ But $\angle B A C=45^{\circ}$ $\angle C A X=90^{\circ}$ So, the equation of $A C$ is $\frac{x-2}{\cos 90^{\circ}}=\frac{y-9}{\sin 90^{\circ}}=r \text { (say) }$ We have $A B=\sqrt{(3-2)^2+(1-0)^2}=\sqrt{2}$
As $A C$ is the new position of $A B$, therefore $A C=A B=\sqrt{2}$ thus, the coordinates of $C$ are given by $\frac{x-2}{\cos 90^{\circ}}=\frac{y-0}{\sin 90^{\circ}}=\sqrt{2}$ $\Rightarrow \quad x=2, y=\sqrt{2}$ Hence, the coordinates of $C$ are $(2, \sqrt{2})$.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mo>≠</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>b</mi><mo>≠</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>c</mi></math> be three real numbers and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>p</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>a</mi><mi>p</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>b</mi><mi>q</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>c</mi></mrow><msqrt><msup><mi>a</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>b</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></msqrt></mfrac><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mo>∀</mo><mo> </mo><mi>p</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>q</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi>R</mi></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi><mfenced><mrow><mfrac><mn>2</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac><mo>,</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><mi>L</mi><mfenced><mrow><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac><mo>,</mo><mfrac><mn>2</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><mi>L</mi><mo>(</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>)</mo><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, then the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>b</mi><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>c</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> always passes through the fixed point
questions.solution:
Given that Lp,q=ap+bq+ca2+b2.

L23,13=a23+b13+ca2+b2=2a+b+3c3a2+b2

L13,23=a13+b23+ca2+b2=a+2b+3c3a2+b2

L2,2=a2+b2+ca2+b2=2a+2b+ca2+b2=6a+6b+3c3a2+b2

L23,13+L13,23+L2,2

=2a+b+3c3a2+b2+a+2b+3c3a2+b2+6a+6b+3c3a2+b2

=9a+9b+9c3a2+b2=0

∴ 9a+9b+9c=0⇒a+b+c=0   ...1

By verifying the options

Substitute (1,1) in the given equation of line,

a1+b1+c=0    ...2
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The lines $2 x+3 y=6,2 x+3 y=8$ cut the $X$-axis at $A$ and $B$, respectively. A line $L$ drawn through the point $(2,2)$ meets the $X$-axis as $C$ in such a way that abscissae of $A, B$ and $C$ are in arithmetic progression. Then, the equation of the line $L$ is
questions.solution:
Given lines are
$2 x+3 y=6$ and $2 x+3 y=8$ These lines meet the $X$-axis at $A(3,0)$ and $B(4,0)$. The line $L$ passes through $(2,2)$ cuts $X$-axis at $C$, such that $x$-coordinate of $A, B, C$ are in ap i.e. $3,4, x_1$ $\begin{array}{ll}\Rightarrow & 2.4=3+x_1 \\ \Rightarrow & x_1=8-3=5\end{array}$ To coordinates of $C$ are $(5,0)$. A line $L$ passing through the points $(2,2)$ and $(5,0)$, is $\begin{aligned} & y-0 & =\frac{0-2}{5-2}(x-5) \\ \Rightarrow & y & =\frac{-2}{3}(x-5) \\ \Rightarrow & 3 y & =-2 x+10 \\ \Rightarrow & 2 x+3 y & =10\end{aligned}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.q: Let the incentre of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">Δ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is <math><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mn>5</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>.</mo></math> If <math><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mn>13</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>, then the sum of the slopes of sides <math><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is
questions.solution:
Slope of AB is 13-11--4=125
Slope of BI is 5-12--4=23
Slope of AI is 13-51-2=-8
Let, the slopes of BC and AC are m1 and m2 respectively
tan⁡A2=tan⁡A2⇒m2--81+m2-8=-8-1251+-8125
m2+891=1-8m252
⇒m2=-43
Now tan⁡B2=tan⁡B2⇒m1-231+m123=23-1251+23×125
⇒3m1-239=3+2m1-26
⇒m1=0
⇒m1+m2=-43
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The locus of the image of a variable point $(\alpha, 2 \alpha-1)$ with respect to the line $3 x-2 y+4=0$, is
questions.solution:
Given a point $\mathrm{P}(\alpha, 2 \alpha-1)$ with respect to the line $3 x-2 y+4=0$. Formula for locus of point $p$ is $$ \frac{\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{x}_1}{\mathrm{a}}=\frac{\mathrm{y}-\mathrm{y}_1}{\mathrm{~b}}=\frac{-2\left(\mathrm{ax}_1+\mathrm{by}_1+\mathrm{c}\right)}{\mathrm{a}^2+\mathrm{b}^2} $$
Here, $\mathrm{x}_1=\alpha, \mathrm{y}_1=2 \alpha-1, \mathrm{a}=3, \mathrm{~b}=-2, \mathrm{c}=4$ $$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{x}-\alpha}{3}=\frac{\mathrm{y}-\mathrm{z} \alpha+1}{-2}=\frac{-2(3(\alpha)+(-2)(2 \alpha-1)+4}{3^2+2^2} \\ & \frac{\mathrm{x}-\alpha}{3}=\frac{\mathrm{y}-\mathrm{z} \alpha+1}{-2}=\frac{-2}{13}(-\alpha+6) \end{aligned} $$ compare variable terms with constant term $$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{x}-\alpha}{3}=\frac{-12}{13}(-\alpha+6) \\ & 13 \mathrm{x}-13 \alpha=-6(-\alpha+6) \\ & 13 \mathrm{x}-13 \alpha=6 \alpha+36 \\ & 13 \mathrm{x}+36=19 \alpha \end{aligned} $$
Now, $$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{y}-2 \alpha+1}{-2}=\frac{-2}{13}(-\alpha+6) \\ & 13 \mathrm{y}-26 \alpha+13=4(-\alpha+6) \\ & 13 \mathrm{y}-26 \alpha+13+4 \alpha-24=0 \end{aligned} $$ $13 y-11==22 \alpha$
compare equation (i) and (ii) $$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{13 x+36}{19}=\frac{13 y-11}{22} \\ & 22(13 x+36)=19(13 y-11) \end{aligned} $$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: A quadrilateral <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi><mi>C</mi><mi>D</mi></math> is divided by the diagonal <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>C</mi></math> into two triangles of equal areas. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>B</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>C</mi></math> are respectively <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mfenced><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mfenced><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>, then the locus of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>D</mi></math> is
questions.solution:
Given points are A(3,4), B(-3,6), C(-5,1), D(x,y).

Here area of ABC=Area of ACD.

Area of ABC=12∑x1y2-y3

=1236-1+-31-4+-54-6

=1235+-3-3+-5-2

=1215+9+10

=17  ...1

Area of ACD=12∑x1y2-y3

=1231-y+-5y-4+x4-1

=123-3y-5y+20+3x

=12+3x-8y+23  ...2

From equations (1) and (2),

±(+3x-8y+23)=34

⇒3x-8y+23=±34

⇒3x-8y+23=34 or 3x-8y+23=-34

⇒3x-8y-11=0 or 3x-8y+57=0

∴ The locus of D is 3x-8y-113x-8y+57=0.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The four distinct points $(0,0),(2,0),(0,-2)$ and $(k,-2)$ are concyclic, if $k$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Let the equation of the circle is
$$
\begin{aligned}
& x^2+y^2+2 g x+2 f y+c=0 \\
& \text { at }(0,0), 0+0+0+0+c=0 \Rightarrow c=0 \\
& \text { at } \quad(2,0), 4+0+2 g \cdot 2+0+0=0 \\
& \Rightarrow \quad g=-1 \\
& \text { at }(0,-2), 0+4+0-4 f+0=0 \Rightarrow f=1 \\
& \text { at }(k,-2), k^2+4+2 k g-4 f+0=0 \\
& \Rightarrow \quad k^2+4-2 k-4=0 \\
& \Rightarrow \quad k(k-2)=0 \\
& \Rightarrow \quad k=0, k=2 \\
&
\end{aligned}
$$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: For all values of $a$ and $b$ the line $(a+2 b) x+(a-b) y+(a+5 b)=0$ passes through the point.
questions.solution:
Let the line passes through a point whose coordinates are $(-2,1)$, then
$\begin{aligned}(a & +2 b)(-2)+(a-b)(1)+a+5 b \\ & =-2 a-4 b+a-b+a+5 b \\ & =-2 a-5 b+2 a+5 b \\ & =0\end{aligned}$
$\therefore$ Our assumption is true. The required point is $(-2,1)$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: If the axes are rotated through an angle $45^{\circ}$ in the positive direction without changing the origin, then the co-ordinates of the point $(\sqrt{2}, 4)$ in the old system are
questions.solution:
If $\theta$ is the angle of rotation, then the co-ordinates in the new system are $x^{\prime}=x \cos \theta+y \sin \theta$, $y^{\prime}=y \cos \theta-x \sin \theta$
Given that $x^{\prime}=\sqrt{2}, y^{\prime}=4$
Thus, $x \cos \theta+y \sin \theta=\sqrt{2}$
$$
y \cos \theta-x \sin \theta=4
$$
Also, $\theta=\frac{\pi}{4} \Rightarrow x \cos \frac{\pi}{4}+y \sin \frac{\pi}{4}=\sqrt{2}$
and $y \cos \frac{\pi}{4}-x \sin \frac{\pi}{4}=4$
$$
\begin{array}{ll}
\Rightarrow & x+y=2 \\
\text { and } & y-x=4 \sqrt{2}
\end{array}
$$
On adding Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get $2 y=2+4 \sqrt{2}$ $\Rightarrow \quad y=1+2 \sqrt{2}$
On subtracting (i) and (ii), we get
$$
\begin{array}{rlrl} 
& & 2 x & =2-4 \sqrt{2} \\
& x & =1-2 \sqrt{2}
\end{array}
$$
$$
\Rightarrow \quad x=1-2 \sqrt{2}
$$
Thus, the co-ordinates of $(\sqrt{2}, 4)$ in the old system $(1-2 \sqrt{2}, 1+2 \sqrt{2})$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: If $(-2, 6)$ is the image of the point $(4, 2)$ with respect to the line $L = 0$, then $L$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Slope of the line $C D$
$$ =\frac{6-2}{-2-4}=\frac{4}{-6}=-\frac{2}{3} $$ Slope of the line $A B=-\frac{1}{-\frac{2}{3}}=\frac{3}{2}$ Mid-point of $C D$ is $O\left(\frac{4-2}{2}, \frac{2+6}{2}\right)$ $$ =O(1,4) $$ Equation of line $A B$ is, $$ \begin{aligned} y-4 & =\frac{3}{2}(x-1) \\ \Rightarrow \quad 2 y-8 & =3 x-3 \\ \Rightarrow \quad 3 x-2 y+5 & =0 \end{aligned} $$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Let $\mathrm{PQR}$ be a right angled isosceles triangle, right angled at $P(2,1)$. If the equation of the side $Q R$ is $2 x+y=3$, then the equation of one of the sides other than $Q R$ is
questions.solution:
Since $\triangle P Q R$ is isosceles right angled triangle. Hence $\angle P Q R=\angle P R Q=45^{\circ}$
Let $m_1$ and $m_2$ be the slope of line $P Q$ and line $P R$ respectively. Given line $Q R$ is $2 x+y=3$ $\Rightarrow y=-2 x+3$. Hence slope of $Q R=m=-2$ Since the angle between $P Q$ and $R Q$ is $45^{\circ}$. Hence $$ \begin{aligned} & \tan 45^{\circ}=\frac{m_1-m}{1+m_1 m}=\frac{m_1+2}{1-2 m_1} \\ & \Rightarrow m_1+2=1-2 m_1 \Rightarrow 3 m_1=-1 \\ & \Rightarrow m_1=-\frac{1}{3} \end{aligned} $$ Since $P R$ and $P Q$ are perpendicular lines Hence $m_1 m_2=-1$ $$ \Rightarrow m_2=-\frac{1}{m_1}=3 \Rightarrow m_2=3 $$ Hence equation of line $P Q$, $$ \begin{aligned} & y-1=m_1(x-2)=\left(-\frac{1}{3}\right)(x-2) \\ & \Rightarrow 3 y+x+1=0 \end{aligned} $$ Hence equation of line $P R$, $$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow y-1=m_2(x-2)=3(x-2) \\ & \Rightarrow 3 x-y-5=0 \end{aligned} $$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: OPQR is a square and M, N are the mid points of the sides PQ and QR respectively. If the ratio of the areas of the square and the triangle OMN is λ : 6, then <math><mfrac><mrow><mi>λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfrac></math> is equal to ________
questions.solution:
We can assume that OP and OR are x–axis and y–axis respectively.

Let OP = a, then area (square OPQR) = a2
Coordinate of M and N are a,a2 and a2,a respectively.
∴ area (∆OMN) = 12aa/2a/2a=3a28
∴ 83=λ6
∴ λ = 16
⇒ λ4=164=4
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The point $\mathrm{P}(2,1)$ is translated to a point $\mathrm{Q}$ parallel to the line $L \equiv x-y-4=0$ by $2 \sqrt{3}$ units. If the point $Q$ lies in the third quadrant, then the equation of the line passing through $\mathrm{Q}$ and perpendicular to $\mathrm{L}$ is
questions.solution:
Given $L \equiv x-y-4=0$ $$ \Rightarrow \frac{x}{(-4)}+\frac{y}{(4)}=1 $$ Slope $m_1$ of line ' $L$ ' is $m_1=1$
Since line ' $L$ ' and line $P Q$ is parallel to each other. Hence slope of line ' $L$ ' $=$ Slope of line $P Q$ Hence equation of $P Q$ can be written as $$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow y-1=m_1(x-2)=(1)(x-2) \\ & \Rightarrow y=(x-1) \\ & \because \text { Distance } P Q=2 \sqrt{3} \\ & \Rightarrow \sqrt{(x-2)^2+(y-1)^2}=2 \sqrt{3} \\ & \Rightarrow(x-2)^2+(x-2)^2=12 \\ & \Rightarrow(x-2)^2=6 \\ & \Rightarrow(x-2)= \pm \sqrt{6} \text { or } x=2+\sqrt{6} \text { or } 2-\sqrt{6} \end{aligned} $$ Here $x=2+\sqrt{6}$ is not valid because point $Q$ lies in third quadrant. Hence $x=2-\sqrt{6}$ Therefore $y=(2-\sqrt{6})-1=1-\sqrt{6}$ $$ \therefore Q(x, y)=Q(2-\sqrt{6}, 1-\sqrt{6}) $$ Let the slope of line perpendicular to given line ' $L$ ' is $m_2$. Then, $$ m_1 m_2=-1 \Rightarrow m_2=-\frac{1}{m_1}=-\frac{1}{1}=-1 $$ Hence equation of line perpendicular to ' $L$ ' and passing through point $Q(2-\sqrt{6}, 1-\sqrt{6})$ will be $$ \begin{aligned} & y-(1-\sqrt{6})=m_2[x-(2-\sqrt{6})]=-1(x-2+\sqrt{6}) \\ & \Rightarrow y-1+\sqrt{6}=-x+2-\sqrt{6} \\ & \Rightarrow x+y=3-2 \sqrt{6} \end{aligned} $$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: If the lines $x+2 a y+a=0, x+3 b y+b=0$, $x+4 c y+c=0$ are concurrent, then $a, b$ and $c$ are in
questions.solution:
Let these three lines be $L_1, L_2$ and $L_3$
$\begin{aligned}
& L_1=x+2 a y+a=0 \\
& L_2=x+3 b y+b=0 \\
& L_3=x+2 c y+c=0
\end{aligned}$
If $L_1, L_2$ and $L_3$ are concurrent, then
$\left|\begin{array}{lll}
1 & 2 a & a \\
1 & 3 b & b \\
1 & 4 c & c
\end{array}\right|=0$
Applying $R_1 \rightarrow R_1-R_2, R_2 \rightarrow R_2-R_3$,
$\begin{aligned}
& \left|\begin{array}{ccc}
0 & 2 a-3 b & a-b \\
0 & 3 b-4 c & b-c \\
1 & 4 c & c
\end{array}\right|=0 \\
& \Rightarrow \quad 1[(2 a-3 b)(b-c)-(3 b-4 c)(a-b)]=0 \\
& \Rightarrow \quad(2 a-3 b)(b-c)=(3 b-4 c)(a-b) \\
& \Rightarrow \quad 2 a b-2 c a-3 b^2+3 b c=3 a b-3 b^2-4 c a+4 b c \\
& \Rightarrow \quad a b+b c=2 c a \\
& \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{a}+\frac{1}{c}=\frac{2}{b}
\end{aligned}$
Hence, $a, b$ and $c$ are in Harmonic progression.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: If the image of the point $(3,8)$ in the line $x+3 y=7$ is $(\alpha, \beta)$, then $\alpha+\beta=$
questions.solution:
Let line $\mathrm{AB}$ be $\mathrm{x}+3 \mathrm{y}=7$ and point $\mathrm{P}$ be $(3,8)$. Let $\mathrm{Q}$ (h, k) be the image of point $P(3,8)$ in the line $x+3 y=7$. Since line $\mathrm{AB}$ is a mirror.
(1) Point $\mathrm{P} \& \mathrm{Q}$ are at equal distance from line $\mathrm{AB}$ i.e. $P R=Q R$ i.e. $R$ is the mid-point of $P Q$.
(2) Image is formed perpendicular to mirror i.e. line $P Q$ is perpendicular to line $\mathrm{AB}$.
Since $\mathrm{R}$ is the midpoint of $\mathrm{PQ}$.
Mid point of $\mathrm{PQ}$ joining $(3,8)$ and $(\mathrm{h}, \mathrm{k})$ is $\left(\frac{\mathrm{h}+3}{2}, \frac{\mathrm{h}+8}{2}\right)$
Coordinate of point $\mathrm{R}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{h}+3}{2}, \frac{\mathrm{h}+8}{2}\right)$
Since point $\mathrm{R}$ lies on the line $\mathrm{AB}$.
Therefore, $\left(\frac{3+\mathrm{h}}{2}\right)+3\left(\frac{8+\mathrm{k}}{2}\right)=7$
$$
\Rightarrow \mathrm{h}+3 \mathrm{k}=-13.....(i)
$$
Also, $\mathrm{PQ}$ is perpendicular to $\mathrm{AB}$
Therefore, slope of $\mathrm{PQ} \times$ Slope of $\mathrm{AB}=-1$
$\Rightarrow$ Slope of $\mathrm{PQ} \times\left(-\frac{1}{3}\right)=-1$
$\Rightarrow$ Slope of $\mathrm{PQ}=3$
Now, $\mathrm{PQ}$ is the line joining $\mathrm{P}(3,8)$ \& $\mathrm{Q}(\mathrm{h}, \mathrm{k})$.
$\therefore$ Slope of $\mathrm{PQ}=3=\frac{\mathrm{k}-8}{\mathrm{~h}-3} \Rightarrow 3 \mathrm{~h}-\mathrm{k}=1$.....(ii)
Solving eqn. (i) \& (ii), we get
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{h}=-1 \& \mathrm{k}=-4 \\
& \therefore \text { Image is } \mathrm{Q}(-1,-4)=(\alpha, \beta) \Rightarrow \alpha=-1, \beta=-4 \\
& \alpha+\beta=-1+(-4)=-5
\end{aligned}
$$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The combined equation of the three sides of a triangle is $\left(x^2-y^2\right)(2 x+3 y-6)=0$. If the point $(0, \alpha)$ lies in the interior of this triangle, then
questions.solution:
The sides of a triangle are given by $$ \left(x^2-y^2\right)(2 x+3 y-6)=0 $$ or $$ x-y=0, x+y=0,2 x+3 y-6=0 $$
We observe that the point $(0, \alpha)$ moves on $x=0$. Thus, the value of $\alpha$ lies between $0 < \alpha < 2$.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Let $\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c}$ and $\mathrm{d}$ be non-zero numbers. If the point of intersection of the lines $4 a x+2 a y+c=0$ and $5 b x+2 b y$ $+\mathrm{d}=0$ lies in the fourth quadrant and is equidistant from the two coordinate axes, then
questions.solution:
Given: $4 \mathrm{ax}+2 \mathrm{ay}+\mathrm{c}=0$
$5 b x+2 b y+d=0$
Eqn. (i) $\times b-$ (ii) $\times a$
$$
-a b x+b c-a d=0
$$
$$
\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=\frac{\mathrm{bc}-\mathrm{ad}}{\mathrm{ab}}
$$
Putting the value of $x$ in eqn. (ii), we get
$$
\begin{aligned}
& 5 b\left(\frac{b c-a d}{a b}\right)+2 b y+d=0 \\
& \Rightarrow y=\frac{4 a d-5 b c}{2 a b}
\end{aligned}
$$
Point of intersection is $P\left(\frac{b c-a d}{a b}, \frac{4 a d-5 b c}{2 a b}\right)$
$\because \mathrm{P}$ is in fourth quadrant and equidistant from the two coordinate axes.
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \therefore-\frac{4 a d-5 b c}{2 a b}=\frac{b c-a d}{a b} \\
& \Rightarrow-4 a d+5 b c=2 b c-2 a d \\
& \Rightarrow 3 b c=2 a b \Rightarrow 3 b c-2 a d=0
\end{aligned}
$$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
